channel.title: Nicotine Science and Policy
channel.description: Daily digest of views, debates and discussions on nicotine science, policy, regulation and advocacy.

item.title: Southern California’s total tobacco bans aren’t helping public health
item.description: Southern California’s total tobacco bans raise serious questions about whether prohibition is improving public health or simply shifting behaviour elsewhere. When legal access is removed, consumers may turn to neighbouring markets, illicit sellers or more harmful products that remain easier to obtain. Policies aimed at reducing harm should be judged by real-world outcomes, not symbolic restrictions. Effective regulation should protect young people while preserving realistic pathways away from combustible tobacco. (Published on July 03, 2026byreason.org)

item.title: What The FDA’s Latest Ruling Could Mean For Future Of Nicotine Pouches
item.description: The FDA’s ruling on nicotine pouches is a major step towards honest relative-risk communication. By recognising that authorised smoke-free products can present lower risks than cigarettes, regulators are acknowledging what tobacco harm reduction has long argued: combustion is the central danger. Adult smokers need clear, accurate information about alternatives that may help them move away from cigarettes. Public health policy should make safer choices easier to understand, not harder to access. (Published on July 03, 2026bydailycaller.com)

item.title: A WHO rate, unseen studies and a U-turn: Maldives makes cigarettes cheaper
item.description: The Maldives’ decision to make cigarettes cheaper raises serious questions about the consistency of tobacco policy. If public health advice is shaped by opaque studies and international pressure while lower-risk alternatives remain restricted, smokers may be left with the most harmful option made more affordable. Reducing harm requires transparent evidence, proportionate regulation and policies that distinguish between combustible tobacco and safer nicotine products. Public health should not protect cigarettes by default. (Published on July 03, 2026bymaldivesindependent.com)

item.title: The ‘Donald Trump Of Fake Science’
item.description: Bad science becomes a public health problem when weak claims are repeated as certainty. In nicotine policy, flawed studies and exaggerated conclusions can distort risk perception, fuel moral panic and push regulation away from evidence. Vaping should be scrutinised, but criticism must be grounded in robust methods, fair comparisons and context on relative risk. Public health loses credibility when advocacy is dressed up as science. (Published on July 03, 2026byplanetofthevapes.co.uk)

item.title: Kenya's Tobacco Control Bill Should Follow Science, Not Fear
item.description: Kenya’s tobacco control bill should be guided by science, not fear. Regulation must protect young people, but it should also recognise the difference between combustible cigarettes and lower-risk nicotine alternatives. If safer products are restricted too heavily, adult smokers may be left with fewer realistic options to quit. Evidence-based policy should reduce harm, preserve informed choice and keep alternatives within legal, regulated markets. (Published on July 03, 2026byworldvapersalliance.com)
